title: Inefficient Investment Waves
abstract: We propose a dynamic model of investment and trade in a market of a specialized technology subject to two main frictions. First, agents cannot raise outside capital. Second, a random group of agents will have the opportunity to invest in new technology and these opportunities are not contractible. The first friction implies the presence of invesment cycles with abundant invesment and low returns in booms and little invesment and high returns in recessions. Only when the second friction is present invesment cycles are constrained inefficient. Often the inefficiency is two-sided with too much invesment in booms and too little in recessions from a social point of view. Interventions targetting only the underinvesment in recessions might make all agents worse off. Also, the two-sided inefficiency typically implies too volatile prices and too frequent realizations of abnormally low prices compared to fundamentals.
summary_en: Why do industries repeatedly cycle through overinvestment booms and underinvestment busts? This paper builds a dynamic model with two frictions: firms can't raise outside capital, and investment opportunities are not contractible. Under these conditions, investment is too high in booms and too low in busts, from a social perspective. Importantly, policies that only address the underinvestment problem (subsidising investment in recessions) can make things worse. Prices are too volatile and abnormally-low-price episodes are too frequent. The paper provides a coherent framework for thinking about cyclical policy interventions in capital-intensive industries.
policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian industrial-policy and state-aid design in capital-intensive sectors (energy, automotive, semiconductors). Key warning: counter-cyclical investment subsidies that only ease the bust can amplify the boom-side overinvestment. Policies that smooth both ends of the cycle do better. Relevant to Ministry of Economic Development and EU state-aid notifications. Caveat: the model is stylised; specific policy calibration requires sectoral frictions to be identified empirically.
